title: Session Hijacking
description: Stealing or forging a user's session token to impersonate them without knowing their password.
fullExplanation: Web applications use session tokens (cookies) to identify authenticated users. If an attacker obtains a valid session token, they can impersonate that user without needing their password. Tokens can be stolen via XSS (document.cookie), network sniffing (HTTP), MITM, or predicted if generated insecurely. Session fixation forces a victim to use an attacker-known session ID.

techniques.name: Network Sniffing
techniques.description: Capture session cookie over unencrypted HTTP on same network

techniques.name: Session Fixation
techniques.description: Force victim to use attacker-known session ID before authentication

techniques.name: Cross-Site Cooking
techniques.description: Exploit cookie scope to inject cookies for other subdomains

techniques.name: Token Prediction
techniques.description: Weak random number generator — predict valid session IDs

keyFacts: HttpOnly flag prevents JavaScript from reading cookies — stops XSS theft
Secure flag ensures cookie only sent over HTTPS
SameSite=Strict prevents cookie from being sent in cross-site requests
Regenerate session ID after login — prevents session fixation
Short session timeouts limit window of opportunity
Bind session to IP/User-Agent — detect token reuse from different context
JWT tokens: verify signature, check expiry, use short-lived tokens

defenses.name: HttpOnly + Secure Cookies
defenses.description: HttpOnly blocks JS access; Secure ensures HTTPS-only transmission

defenses.name: HTTPS Everywhere
defenses.description: Encrypt all traffic — prevent network sniffing

defenses.name: Session Regeneration
defenses.description: Issue new session ID after login — prevent fixation

defenses.name: Short Session Timeout
defenses.description: Expire sessions after inactivity — limit hijacking window
